narrative_blocks.header: She married a musician deacon in Vermont
narrative_blocks.body: Clarissa Hooker left Connecticut and married Moses Ingersoll at Poultney, Vermont — a man the Ingersoll genealogy describes as a musician, choir director, and deacon. The family moved west, living first in Ashtabula County (Ohio's Western Reserve) and eventually in Augusta, Illinois, where Clarissa died in November 1851. She was 62.

narrative_blocks.header: She married a musician deacon in Vermont
narrative_blocks.body: Clarissa Hooker left Connecticut and married Moses Ingersoll at Poultney, Vermont — a man the Ingersoll genealogy describes as a musician, choir director, and deacon. The family moved west, living first in Ashtabula County (Ohio's Western Reserve) and eventually in Augusta, Illinois, where Clarissa died in November 1851. She was 62.

narrative_blocks.header: When he died, his whole family left
narrative_blocks.body: Moses Ingersoll led the choir for many years, played the bass viol, and taught music throughout the community. As a deacon he often read the sermon — 'such a fine reader many preferred him to the preacher.' When he died, every member of his family left the choir simultaneously, and the congregation had to send to another town to find singers for his own funeral.

bio.notes: Given name "Harriet Hooker" — honoring maternal Hooker ancestry

narrative_blocks.header: Her voice never grew squeaky with age
narrative_blocks.body: Lydia Talmadge Ingersoll inherited her father Moses's musical talent. Trained by a Boston teacher, her voice grew stronger, not weaker, with age. 'The people would not hear to her giving up her singing.' She lived at Warsaw, Illinois.

Gus Hooker Loder was born in Warsaw, Illinois on January 16, 1859. His mother Lydia Talmadge Ingersoll was the daughter of Moses and Clarissa Hooker Ingersoll — and the Ingersoll family continued to honor the Hooker name by passing it as a given name. By 1859, the Hooker surname had traveled from Hartford to Vermont to Ohio to Illinois and appeared as a first name given to a child named Loder.

narrative_blocks.header: Found dead with his watch in his hand
narrative_blocks.body: William Ingersoll was yard master at Fort Scott, Kansas, making up a train, when he stepped back and his foot caught. The train ran over him, cutting off both legs. He was found dead with his watch in his hand.

migration.notes: Among the first settlers of Bethlehem Parish, Woodbury. One of the leading men of the town for many years.

narrative_blocks.header: Among the first settlers of Bethlehem Parish
narrative_blocks.body: Around 1720 he was among the first settlers of Bethlehem Parish in Woodbury, Connecticut. He was described as 'for many years one of the leading men of the town.'

Edward Hooker's record says he 'died suddenly' in February 1756, at age sixty-seven. The unexpected death cut short his role as one of the leading men of Bethlehem Parish — a settlement he had helped establish thirty-five years before.

bio.notes: Also spelled Curtiss in some records
